target.name: Cartesia
target.founder: Karan Goel, Albert Gu, Arjun Desai, Brandon Yang, Chris Ré

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $186M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 6 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $3.2B - $39.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Physical Intelligence

private.name: Black Forest Labs

data.narrative:
Cartesia is a Series B AI & software company that has raised $186M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a peer set of six same-sector, same/adjacent-stage companies spanning Series A to Series C, including Physical Intelligence ($5.6B Series B), Reflection AI ($8.0B Series A), and Figure AI ($39.0B Series C), alongside one public comparable (Palantir).

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, using the $186M total raised as a proxy for current valuation and applying a typical 1.9x Series B step-up multiplier, implies $353M. Peer-median analysis across the six-company comp set yields $6.8B, with a range spanning $3.2B to $39.0B. The wide peer dispersion reflects heterogeneity in capital efficiency, market timing, and competitive positioning within frontier AI.

The triangulated analysis supports a range of $300M to $7.8B, with a median of $3.6B. The stage-progression method floors the valuation substantially below peer medians, suggesting Cartesia's capital efficiency may be modest relative to peers or that raised capital alone is an insufficient proxy for current market value. The peer-median approach anchors to observable market transactions but carries execution risk given the volatility and early-stage nature of the frontier AI sector. Key uncertainties include product-market fit validation, competitive differentiation, and near-term customer concentration relative to the sampled peer companies.
